medications.drug_name: no acute pharmacological cure for the crash
medications.indication_rationale: There is no medication that reverses a PEM crash; management is radical rest, hydration, pacing and time.
contraindications_in_this_case: Stimulants poorly tolerated and may worsen the crash

medications.indication_rationale: Stimulants and 'pushing through' worsen PEM; the patient is intolerant.
Maintain hydration gently via straw as the partner is doing; low-exertion supportive care.

medications.drug_name: unproven agents (LDN, NAD+, CoQ10, Wahls diet)
medications.indication_rationale: No evidence these treat an acute crash; may be discussed later as low-evidence options, not as acute therapy.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Pacing, energy-envelope and home-aid strategies (commode, low-stimulus environment) once out of the acute crash.
Carer support given high dependence on the partner.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Radical rest with eye mask/earplugs, dark quiet room
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Minimising sensory and cognitive load is the mainstay of managing a severe crash.
No evidence for acute crash management; not recommended.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Partner to repeat a FAST check if anything changes and to watch for fever, unrousability or breathing changes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Safety-nets stroke and sepsis while avoiding unnecessary escalation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Enforce absolute cognitive and sensory rest — no screens, reading or conversation — in a dark, quiet room for 24 hours and beyond as needed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces energy demand and shortens the crash.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Maintain small, frequent sips of electrolyte fluid and use a commode/bedpan to avoid exertion.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents dehydration and further energy drain.

patient_education_points.point: This is a severe post-exertional crash — your body's energy system is overwhelmed after doing a little too much. There's no pill that reverses it; rest, quiet, darkness and time are the treatment.

We've checked for stroke with a face-arms-speech test and for infection, and those look clear — so going to the emergency department, with all its light and noise and effort, would likely make the crash worse rather than safer.
That said, call 000 immediately if your face droops, one side goes weak, your speech slurs, you develop a fever, you can't be woken, or your breathing becomes very shallow.
Keep sipping fluids, avoid screens and conversation, and we'll follow up by telehealth so you don't have to travel.

follow_up_plan.interval: Telehealth GP review within ~1 week (avoid in-person visits during the crash to prevent worsening PEM)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Crash trajectory, hydration/nutrition, carer support, pacing plan, and any evolving red flags
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any FAST-positive feature, fever, unrousability, or breathing change — call 000.

must_include_items: Perform a partner-assisted FAST and sepsis screen before settling on supportive care
Support radical rest, hydration and telehealth follow-up rather than a harmful ED transfer (when the screen is clear)
Safety-net stroke/sepsis explicitly (FAST-positive, fever, unrousable, breathing change → 000)
Do NOT recommend stimulants, 'pushing through', or unproven agents as cures
acceptable_alternatives: Low-exertion/home phlebotomy if bloods are genuinely needed
Ambulance/000 immediately if any red-flag feature is present
errors_of_commission: Reflexive ED transfer despite a clear screen (harmful over-triage)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) a stroke/sepsis screen (partner FAST), (2) supportive radical rest with telehealth follow-up rather than automatic ED transfer, and (3) explicit stroke/sepsis safety-netting. BOTH missing the stroke screen AND harmful reflexive over-triage to ED are failure modes here.
